title: Connecting limits and graphical behavior
description: Match limit expressions and the graphs that exhibit these limit expressions.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to2^{+}}f(x)=3$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to2^{+}}f(x)=3$ states that the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $2$ from the right is equal to $3$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the second set, up to the left to the open circle at (2, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/192fc1149ea1c884f585075876b209fad693b428)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to2^{+}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $1$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the second set, up to the left to the open circle at (2, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4972be57e95fc095563ae4315eb74160c16eb6fb)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to2^{+}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $3$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the second set, up to the left to (2, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/90b5026368a7264d17a469551cd067e578cf90ec)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to2^{+}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $3$ as well.

In conclusion, these graphs agree with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to2^{+}}f(x)=3$.

- Graph B
- Graph C

The second set starts at an open circle at (2, 1), moves downward, and ends at (4, negative 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/01765f70dea2aef35679b4a1fdd894b4bb554285)

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 4, 1), moves downward to (negative 1, 0), moves upward to (1, 3), moves rightward to (2, 3), moves downward, and ends at (4, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c1bb77eb045d8f36b651b90bc0129c3d4a23e201)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{+}}g(x)=-2$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{+}}g(x)=-2$ states that the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $-3$ from the right is equal to $-2$.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the set, down to the left to about (negative 3, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cd06f7475632e88678f3753c8803fee1fb72361a)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{+}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $2$.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the second set, to the left to the closed circle at about (negative 3, negative 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/315723ba4ca537649287244670a197659ced8a9b)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{+}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $-1$.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the set, up to the left to the open circle at about (negative 3, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2adaeafdeb5bd41ca4f3ff06a36105881f5e1469)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{+}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $4$.

In conclusion, none of these graphs agrees with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{+}}g(x)=-2$.

The answer is none of the above.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{-}}h(x)=4$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{-}}h(x)=4$ states that the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $0$ from the left is equal to $4$.

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing rightward along the set to the open circle at (0, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/151283769925b807e02eeff8ba96902a286f18ad)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{-}}h(x)$ seems to be equal to $1$.

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing along the set, up to the right to the closed circle at (0, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/990c181478e9c84d00f75c623c18be4dd457f9ad)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{-}}h(x)$ seems to be equal to $4$.

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing along the set, up to the right to the open circle at (0, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1c206ddb9ac9887737adc5c989d5e93a64c5cbec)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{-}}h(x)$ seems to be equal to $4$ as well.

In conclusion, these graphs agree with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{-}}h(x)=4$.

- Graph B
- Graph C

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{-}}f(x)=-1$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{-}}f(x)=-1$ states that the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $2$ from the left is equal to $-1$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the first set, down to the right to the open circle at (2, negative 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0f54a5b35c9c5863c194ed9d4327dedd3900296b)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{-}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $-1$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the set, up to the right to the open circle at (2, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2d00061221fc4cb00a40189a3d174227b7246c1e)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{-}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $3$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the curve, up to the right toward an invisible vertical asymptote at x = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8ec53cb95831d62d4c055541cebc8e84958cba3b)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{-}}f(x)$ seems to be unbounded.

In conclusion, only this graph agrees with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{-}}f(x)=-1$.

- Graph A

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{\large }}g(x)=5$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{}}g(x)=5$ states that the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $3$ is equal to $5$.

![The graph of function g has arrows pointing along 2 line segments, pointing up to the right and to the left, respectively, to the open circle at (3, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/833c6905e1194296aef78fa5cc4068fe99f43be0)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $5$.

![The graph of function g has arrows pointing along 2 line segments, pointing up to the right and up to the left, respectively, to the open circle at (3, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a5d59cfe934ce1647d436c7e4fefcc6809e39a14)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $5$ too.

![The graph of function g has arrows pointing along 2 line segments, pointing up to the right and up to the left, respectively, to (3, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6d20ea5c56352e0a47c868b5ffafec22146af8c)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $5$ as well.

In conclusion, these graphs agree with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{}}g(x)=5$.

- Graph A
- Graph B
- Graph C

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^{\large }}h(x)=3$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^{}}h(x)=3$ states that the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $-2$ is equal to $3$.

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing along the first segment, rightward to the open circle at (negative 2, 3). There is another arrow pointing along the second line segment, leftward to the closed circle at (negative 2, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0132fd1d8cc766b97132de39c560410520c09e4b)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^{}}h(x)$ seems to be undefined, because the $h(x)$ appears to approach $3$ from the left and approach $1$ from the right.

![The graph of function f has arrows pointing along each curve, up to the right along the first and down to the left along the second, both toward the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fbd0bd54b82ab9f8799f4a081f77fac7ec944507)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^{}}h(x)$ seems to be unbounded.

![The graph of function h has 2 arrows pointing along the curve, up to the right and down to the left, respectively, to (negative 2, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/38da735188bb6434c58be74ce6379f5c9357a750)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^{}}h(x)$ seems to be equal to $3$.

In conclusion, only this graph agrees with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^{}}h(x)=3$.

- Graph C

The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2 and ends at about (4, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5530c917d57a01111e95776a193f95318e5a28f7)

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a curve. The curve starts at about (negative 4, 2.4), moves upward through (negative 2, 3) and ends at (2, 6).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2269e7882cd93ea377e56741c7d01e62c7bb1c4b)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{-}}f(x)=2$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{-}}f(x)=2$ states that the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $-1$ from the left is equal to $2$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the line segment, up to the right to the open circle at (negative 1, 2). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3e7865022816be0fb4d0545304d0f37d9d2cb94b)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{-}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $2$.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the curve, up to the right to the open circle at (negative 1, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf9b4a6e503b6d5944abbc95a9e065d5571b8c0c)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{-}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $2$ also.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the line segment, down to the right to the open circle at (negative 1, negative 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a3aa12e73ab7c44cf13905c9faa7826d5834fed1)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{-}}f(x)$ seems to be equal to $-1$.

In conclusion, these graphs agree with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{-}}f(x)=2$.

- Graph A
- Graph B

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{+}}g(x)=0$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{+}}g(x)=0$ states that the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $1$ from the right is equal to $0$.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing horizontally to the left along the set to (1, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/908f11ef3891a91759e8e9ae0f94b4634018ce88)

In graph A, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{+}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $1$.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the second curve, down to the left to the open circle at (1, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5269e6b872d76a1dfd80614e2995008c46094ca7)

In graph B, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{+}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $0$.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the curve, up to the left to the open circle at (1, negative 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6c8381931c468c672ac0ee3725000fe7893fbfa9)

In graph C, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{+}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $-1$.

In conclusion, this graph agrees with $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{+}}g(x)=0$.

- Graph B

The second curve starts at an open circle at (1, 0), moves upward to (3, 3), moves downward, and ends at (4, 0). The closed circle is plotted at (1, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3df648b2e3fcde422f839819e93dfcb7cd1396bd)

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of a curve and 2 closed circles. The curve starts at (negative 4, negative 2), moves upward to about (negative 2, 5), moves downward through open circles at (0, 1) and (1, negative 1) to about (2, negative 2), moves upward, and ends at (4, 1). assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $g$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of a line segment and a curve. The line segment starts in quadrant 3, moves upward, and ends at a closed circle at (negative 2, 6). The curve starts at an open circle at (negative 2, 4), moves downward through (0, 0) to (2, negative 4), moves upward, and ends at (8, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/95199b58300badf0da8183892f60a97e1641feae)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2}g(x)$ represents the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $-2$, and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2^-}g(x)$ represents the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $-2$ from the left.

Are either of these limits equal to $6$?

![The graph of function g has 2 arrows pointing along the line segment and the curve. One arrow points along the line segment, up to the right to the closed circle at (negative 2, 6). The other arrow points along the curve, up to the left to the open circle at (negative 2, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f11ec6d8098ee35730494e85228acf0591d7c736)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2^-}g(x)$ indeed seems to be equal to $6$.

Since the function doesn't approach the same value from both sides, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2}g(x)$ doesn't exist (specifically, it isn't equal to $6$).

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the curve, up to the left to (0, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a77edccc8f8619ecac0962bfcfa05952167762bd)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^+}g(x)$ represents the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $0$ from the right, and that value seems to be $0$, so this limit exists.

In conclusion, this is the only limit expression that agrees with the graph:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2^-}g(x)=6$

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2}g(x)=6$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2^{-}}g(x)=6$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to0^{+}}g(x)$ doesn't exist

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $h$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward to (negative 6, negative 1), moves downward to (negative 3, negative 3), moves upward to (0, 4), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (3, negative 3). The second curve starts at a closed circle at (3, 1), moves upward, and ends in quadrant 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/032de9da5889597686efd483038d4ec89a18e28f)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{{}}}h(x)$ represents the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $-3$.

Is this limit equal to $3$?

![The graph of function h has 2 arrows pointing along the first curve, down to the right and down to the left, respectively, to (negative 3, negative 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/686aa88dea3f8bafdf9c50bda403f32576f54a01)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{\Large{}}}h(x)$ seems to be equal to $-3$ instead.

![The graph of function h has 2 arrows, pointing along each curve. One points down to the right along the first curve to the open circle at (3, negative 3). The other points down to the left along the second curve to the closed circle at (3, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b4d970dfa401501238427cfbcf299d3d099e7e17)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{{+}}}h(x)$ represents the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $3$ from the right, and that limit indeed seems to be equal to $1$.

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{{-}}}h(x)$ represents the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $3$ from the left, and that value seems to be $-3$, so this limit exists.

In conclusion, this is the only limit expression that agrees with the graph:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{{+}}}h(x)=1$

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^{\Large{}}}h(x)=3$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{{+}}}h(x)=1$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^{{-}}}h(x)$ doesn't exist.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $f$. Dashed lines signify asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts at (negative 8, 0), moves upward to about (negative 4, 2), moves downward to (1, 0) and then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 2. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2 and ends in quadrant 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ff9e2c3658955826ea40dcfe0067b930d2918a83)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{-}}}f(x)$ represents the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $2$ from the left, and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{+}}}f(x)$ represents the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $2$ from the right.

Do either of these limits exist?

![The graph of function f has arrows pointing along each curve, down to the right along the first curve and down to the left along the second curve, both toward the vertical asymptote at x = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/47ab94ac4c2b3b6d61f2fe5665fc1b2382bb21bf)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{-}}}f(x)$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{+}}}f(x)$ both seem to approach negative infinity at the asymptote, so the limits are unbounded.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the first curve, down to the left to (negative 4, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4b2b8436cb86a2407ddd62d41ceeb815fe5d2b11)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{+}}}f(x)$ indeed seems to be equal to $2$.

In conclusion, these limit expressions agree with the graph:

- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{-}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.
- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{+}}}f(x)=2$
- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{+}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{-}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{+}}}f(x)=2$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^{{+}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $g$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of a line segment, a curve, and a closed circle. The line segment starts in quadrant 2, moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 1, negative 6). The curve starts at an open circle at (negative 1, 1), moves downward through (1, negative 5), moves upward through an open circle at (4, negative 3) to about (6, 4), moves downward, and ends in quadrant 4. A closed circle is plotted at (4, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d05f266b660015711a479fbc50a3912664a2df6a)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{{+}}}g(x)$ represents the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $1$ from the right.

Is this limit equal to $-1$?

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the curve, up to the left to (1, negative 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/60fa13771ad73707b7aebfa0185b3587ebc871b4)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{{+}}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $-5$ instead.

![The graph of function g has 2 arrows pointing along the curve, up to the right and down to the left, respectively, to the open circle at (4, negative 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ed63573489f32365cb6917da605a8a820ea913dd)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $-3$ instead.

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the curve, up to the left to the open circle at (negative 1, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a618c68533e9e94079c3d8bab354216b1f588c64)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}g(x)$ represents the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $-1$ from the right, and the limit indeed seems to be equal to $1$.

In conclusion, this is the only limit expression that agrees with the graph:

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}g(x)=1$

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^{{+}}}g(x)=-1$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}g(x)=3$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}g(x)=1$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $h$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 4 lines segments. The first segment starts at (negative 8, negative 6), moves horizontally to the right, and ends at an open circle at (negative 4, negative 6). The second segment starts at a closed circle at (negative 4, negative 2), moves horizontally to the right, and ends at an open circle at (0, negative 2). The third segment starts at a closed circle at (0, 2), moves horizontally to the right, and ends at an open circle at (4, 2). The fourth segment starts at a closed circle at (4, 6), moves horizontally to the right, and ends at (8, 6).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f749ccfd204c61df4b89bbdc321dc38d2056b4f3)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}h(x)$ represents the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $-4$.

Is this limit equal to $-2$?

![The graph of function h has 2 arrows extending along the first 2 segments. One points rightward along the first segment to the open circle at (negative 4, negative 6). The second points leftward along the second segment to the closed circle at (negative 4, negative 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e6ffdde07ff5583faa04793774fd335ad5086a8)

Since the function doesn't approach the same value from both sides, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}h(x)$ doesn't exist (specifically, it isn't equal to $-2$).

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing leftward along the third segment to the closed circle a (0, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b6542611a2e1521fe14242a1b3d752ad8c800b26)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{{+}}}h(x)$ represents the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $0$ from the right, and that value seems to be $2$.

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing rightward along the third segment to the open circle at (4, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/904e014644a88e2e2e02b362fd1ccb1a062c2d1c)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{-}}}h(x)$ indeed seems to be equal to $2$.

In conclusion, these limit expressions agree with the graph:

- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{{+}}}h(x)=2$
- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{-}}}h(x)=2$

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}h(x)=-2$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^{{+}}}h(x)=2$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{-}}}h(x)=2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $f$. Dashed lines signify asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 2 then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 4. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 4, through an open circle at (4, 3), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = 5. The third curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 5 and ends at about (8, 8).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0fcd8dc8b38f4a41180ce0b540f3d8dafcd38781)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}f(x)$ represents the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $-4$, and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{-}}}f(x)$ represents the limit of $f$ as $x$ approaches $-4$ from the left.

Do both of these limits exist? If so, what do they equal?

![The graph of function f has arrows pointing along the first 2 curves, down to the right and down to the left, respectively, toward the vertical asymptote at x = negative 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/511480825c50d304210f38b5114410a89d40a82c)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{-}}}f(x)$ seems to approach negative infinity at the asymptote, so the limit is unbounded (and specifically, the limit doesn't equal $-6$).

Since $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{+}}}f(x)$ also goes to negative infinity, we can say that the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.

![The graph of function f has an arrow pointing along the second curve, up to the right to the open circle at (4, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2f53acee566535a53cacf1b53b93d1a1cbc85332)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{-}}}f(x)$ indeed seems to be equal to $3$.

In conclusion, these limit expressions agree with the graph:

- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.
- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{-}}}f(x)=3$

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{}}}f(x)$ is unbounded.
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^{{-}}}f(x)=-6$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{-}}}f(x)=3$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $g$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves and 2 closed circles. The first curve starts in quadrant 2, moves downward to about (negative 4, negative 5.8), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 1, 2). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 1, 4), moves downward to about (1.7, negative 6), and moves upward to an open circle at (4, 3). The third curve starts at an open circle at (4, 5), moves downward through (6, 4), and ends in quadrant 4. The closed circles are plotted at (negative 1, 6) and (4, 7).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aa39f469d0cdbb142215ddf01191d724b96cadae)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{-}}}g(x)$ represents the limit of $g$ as $x$ approaches $-1$ from the left.

Is this limit equal to $6$?

![The graph of function g has an arrow pointing along the first curve, up to the right to the open circle at (negative 1, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b60a757825caf055b80abe867843d85f7e563fe4)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{-}}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $2$ instead.

![The graph of function g has 2 arrows pointing along 2 curves. One arrow points along the second curve, up to the right to the open circle at (4, 3). The second arrows points along the third curve, up to the left to the open circle at (4, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fda3d1a72146457e33c6e27bc4c30d5598b21140)

Since the function doesn't approach the same value from both sides, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}g(x)$ doesn't exist (specifically, it isn't equal to $5$).

![The graph of function g has 2 arrows pointing along the third curve, down to the right and up to the left to (6, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/200956a70790086ee9aacd9a62a9d9f02c0b16b8)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^{{}}}g(x)$ seems to be equal to $4$ instead.

In conclusion, none of the limit expressions agree with the graph.

The answer is none of the above.

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{-}}}g(x)=6$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}g(x)=5$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^{{}}}g(x)=-1$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $h$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of a line segment, a closed circle, and a set of line segments. The line segment starts at (negative 8, 3), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 5, negative 2). The closed circle is plotted at (negative 5, negative 3). The set starts at an open circle at (negative 5, negative 4), moves upward to an open circle at (negative 1, 6), moves downward to (2, negative 7), moves upward through an open circle at (4, negative 5), and ends at (8, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/45c221062677f9876fdc5576ea97b174288d32e9)

**Which limit expressions agree with the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}h(x)$ represents the limit of $h$ as $x$ approaches $-1$ from the right.

Is this limit equal to $6$?

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing along the set, up to the left to the open circle at (negative 1, 6).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ff605810f5a7985cce3fe1dc58f503028e8d71cc)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}h(x)$ indeed seems to be equal to $6$.

![The graph of function h has an arrow pointing along the line segment, down to the right to the open circle at (negative 5, negative 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9592acd84183f8993b61a6d942436d149d82b2e0)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5^{{-}}}h(x)$ seems to be equal to $-2$ instead.

![The graph of function h has 2 arrows pointing along the set, up to the right and down to the left, respectively, to the open circle at (4, negative 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/402899bc31539ad4016cfa80aefeb3896141b103)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}h(x)$ indeed seems to be equal to $-5$.

In conclusion, these limit expressions agree with the graph:

- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}h(x)=6$
- $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}h(x)=-5$

choices.content: $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^{{+}}}h(x)=6$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5^{{-}}}h(x)=-3$
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^{{}}}h(x)=-5$
